subject: Mathematics
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 1 period = 40 minutes
Total: 1 periods · 40 minutes

textbook_items_in_class.description: Draw two lines on a plain sheet that intersect. Measure the four angles with a protractor. Repeat for four such pairs. Observe patterns among the angles and reason whether vertically opposite angles are always equal.
Can two straight lines intersect at more than one point?
What patterns do you observe among the four angles formed when two lines intersect (after Activity 1)?

phases.description: Introduce two lines meeting at a point, elicit prior knowledge of angles. Ask students to predict how many angles form at an intersection.
Students conduct Activity 1 (p.107) ("draw two lines on a plain sheet that intersect...."). Record angle measurements in a table; note patterns.

Discuss Figure it Out, section 5.1 p.107 ("can two straight lines intersect at more than one point?"). Then Figure it Out, section 5.1 p.107 ("what patterns do you observe among the four angles....").
Consolidate vocabulary: linear pair (sum 180°), vertically opposite angles (equal). Preview that the next period will prove these relationships deductively.

periods.teacher_notes: Recap students' prior knowledge of angles and straight lines before starting. Watch for protractor misreading — students may measure the wrong arc; emphasise placing the centre on the vertex. For self-study, direct students to Figure it Out section 5.1 p.107 (the ∠a = 120° question).

textbook_items_in_class.description: In Fig. 5.2, if ∠a is 120°, figure out the measurements of ∠b, ∠c, and ∠d without drawing and measuring them.
Is it always true that vertically opposite angles are equal for any pair of intersecting lines? Check for different measures of ∠a and reason whether the property holds generally.

textbook_items_in_class.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 5.1 p.108
textbook_items_in_class.description: List all linear pairs and pairs of vertically opposite angles in Fig. 5.3 (two intersecting lines forming angles a, b, c, d).

phases.description: Recap Period 1 observations: linear pair sum and equal opposite angles. Pose the question: can we prove these without measuring?

Walk through the deductive chain: ∠a + ∠b = 180° and ∠a + ∠d = 180°, therefore ∠b = ∠d; name this a proof. Discuss the Measurements and Geometry interlude on why measured values deviate from theoretical ones.

Students attempt Figure it Out, section 5.1 p.107 ("in Fig. 5.2, if ∠a is 120°, figure out ∠b, ∠c, ∠d...."). Then Figure it Out Q1, section 5.1 p.108 ("list all linear pairs and pairs of vertically opposite angles....").
Discuss answers and consolidate: vertical-opposite equality holds for any ∠a by the same algebraic argument. Preview perpendicular lines as a special intersection case.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: connect the empirical patterns from Period 1 to the algebraic proof introduced here. A common error is confusing adjacent angles with vertically opposite ones — use labelled diagrams on the board. For self-study, point students to Figure it Out section 5.1 p.107 (the 'Is it always true?' question).

periods.activity_title: Right Angles, Parallel Lines, and Real-World Lines

textbook_items_in_class.description: Observe Fig. 5.5 and describe how the various line segments meet using mathematical vocabulary (point, endpoint, midpoint, meet, intersect) and degree measures. Are line segments ST and UV likely to meet if extended?
Are line segments OP and QR in Fig. 5.5 likely to meet if extended?
Which pairs of lines appear to be parallel in Fig. 5.6 (dot grid with lines a through i)?

phases.description: Define perpendicular lines as the special intersection where all four angles equal 90°; introduce the small-square notation. Ask students for real-world examples (walls, ruled paper).

Transition to section 5.3. Figure it Out, section 5.3 p.109 ("observe Fig. 5.5 and describe how line segments meet...."). Figure it Out, section 5.3 p.109 ("are line segments OP and QR likely to meet if extended?").
Present the formal definition of parallel lines (coplanar, never meet); emphasise coplanarity. Figure it Out, section 5.3 p.110 ("which pairs of lines appear to be parallel in Fig. 5.6?").
Consolidate vocabulary: perpendicular (⊥), parallel (∥), coplanar. Preview paper-folding activities that will apply both concepts next period.

periods.teacher_notes: Recap the vertically-opposite result from Period 2 before introducing the 90° special case. A common error is thinking non-meeting lines in 3D (e.g., skew lines on different planes) are parallel — stress coplanarity. For self-study, encourage students to re-read the teacher note in section 5.3 about lines on different surfaces.
materials: Textbook open to Fig. 5.5 and Fig. 5.6

textbook_segments.title: Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Paper Folding

periods.activity_title: Folding Paper to Build Parallel and Perpendicular Lines

textbook_items_in_class.description: Fold a square sheet of paper horizontally in half multiple times; count parallel lines formed (pattern: 2ⁿ + 1 after n folds). Make a vertical fold and observe it is perpendicular to previous folds. Fold along a diagonal and find a parallel crease.

Fold a square sheet to make a centre crease, fold edges to centre, then fold top-right and bottom-left corners onto the crease to form triangles. Reason whether crease lines a, b, c are parallel to p, q, r respectively.

textbook_items_in_class.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 5.4 p.113
textbook_items_in_class.description: Draw some lines perpendicular to the given lines on the dot paper in Fig. 5.10.

homework.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 5.4 p.113
homework.description: In Fig. 5.11, mark parallel lines with arrow notation and perpendicular lines with a square symbol. (a) How did you spot the perpendicular lines? (b) How did you spot the parallel lines?

phases.description: Distribute square sheets. Recap: parallel lines never meet; perpendicular lines meet at 90°. Students predict what folding horizontally will create.
Activity 2 (p.111) ("fold a square sheet of paper horizontally in half multiple times; count parallel lines...."). Then make a vertical fold and observe it is perpendicular to previous folds.
Activity 2 (corner folds) (p.112) ("fold a square sheet to make a centre crease, fold edges to centre...."). Reason whether crease lines a, b, c are parallel to p, q, r.

Figure it Out Q1, section 5.4 p.113 ("draw some lines perpendicular to the given lines on the dot paper."). Introduce arrow and square notations. Assign Figure it Out Q2, section 5.4 p.113 ("in Fig. 5.11, mark parallel lines with arrow notation....") as homework.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: connect the paper creases directly to the definitions of parallel and perpendicular recalled in Period 3. Watch for students who fold imprecisely and get non-parallel creases — remind them that the mathematical ideal requires exact alignment. For self-study, direct students to Figure it Out Q3 p.113.
materials: Square sheets of paper (one per student)

textbook_items_in_class.description: Is it possible for all eight angles formed by a transversal crossing two lines to have different measurements? Why or why not?
What about five different angle measures — is that possible for the eight angles formed?

phases.description: Review homework from Period 4 (Q2 p.113). Define a transversal as a line cutting two other lines, forming 8 angles total. Ask students to draw an example and label all 8 angles.

Figure it Out, section 5.5 p.115 ("is it possible for all eight angles formed by a transversal to have different measurements?"). Guide application of vertically opposite pairs; establish 4 distinct values maximum.
Figure it Out, section 5.5 p.115 ("what about five different angle measures — is that possible?"). Students explain why not, reinforcing the 4-distinct-values ceiling.
Consolidate transversal angle vocabulary: name the 4 pairs of vertically opposite angles and introduce the positional idea of 'corresponding' angles as a preview for Period 6.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: prior periods established vertically opposite angles; this period applies that result to a transversal's 8 angles. A common error is believing all 8 angles can differ — emphasise that vertically opposite pairs are always equal, capping distinct values at 4. For self-study, direct students to re-examine their labelled diagrams and verify by substituting specific angle values.

periods.activity_title: Corresponding Angles and the Parallel Condition

textbook_items_in_class.description: Draw line l and transversal t intersecting at X with ∠a = 60°. Mark point Y on t and draw line m through Y at 60° to t (matching ∠a). Observe that l and m appear parallel; verify that corresponding angles are equal.

Given parallel lines l and m with transversal t (Fig. 5.19), trace ∠a on tracing paper and place it over ∠b to check that corresponding angles are equal. Verify all four corresponding angle pairs with a protractor.

Given non-parallel lines l and m (Fig. 5.20), attempt to draw a transversal t such that one pair of corresponding angles is equal. Observe that this is impossible, confirming the necessary condition for parallelism.

phases.description: Recap transversal angle labelling from Period 5. Define corresponding angles by their positional match at each intersection. Ask: what might be special about them when lines are parallel?
Activity 3 (p.115-116) ("draw line l and transversal t intersecting at X with ∠a = 60°...."). Observe that l and m appear parallel; corresponding angles are equal.

Activity 4 (p.117) ("given parallel lines l and m with transversal t, trace ∠a on tracing paper...."). Then Activity 5 (p.117) ("given non-parallel lines l and m, attempt to draw a transversal t....").
Consolidate the biconditional: corresponding angles equal if and only if lines are parallel. Preview that Period 7 will use this criterion to construct parallel lines with instruments.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: connect the maximum-4-distinct-values result from Period 5 to the specific positional pairing of corresponding angles in section 5.6. Watch for students who confuse co-interior (same-side) angles with corresponding ones — insist on the positional definition. For self-study, direct students to re-read the biconditional summary at the end of section 5.6.

periods.activity_title: Constructing Parallel Lines with Set Square and Folding

textbook_items_in_class.description: Can you draw a line parallel to l that goes through point A (Fig. 5.23)? Describe your method using tools from your geometry box.
Why are lines l and m parallel to each other in the paper-folding construction of Fig. 5.24?

phases.description: Recap corresponding-angles criterion from Period 6. Pose the construction challenge: given a line l, draw a line through a given point parallel to l using geometry box tools.
Demonstrate and then have students perform the set-square method: draw perpendiculars to l by sliding the set square, establishing 90° corresponding angles and thus two parallel lines.
Students perform the paper-folding method (double perpendicular through point A). Then Figure it Out, section 5.7 p.119 ("can you draw a line parallel to l through point A?").

Figure it Out, section 5.7 p.119-120 ("why are lines l and m parallel in the paper-folding construction of Fig. 5.24?"). Students write their own explanation articulating double-perpendicular reasoning.
Whole-class sharing of written explanations; correct common misconceptions. Preview the upcoming session on alternate angles as a further angle-relationship tool for parallel lines.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: Period 6 established the corresponding-angles criterion; this period applies it in two construction methods. A common error is sliding the set square without keeping it flush against a baseline — emphasise the baseline must act as a transversal with 90° angles. For self-study, direct students to re-examine Figure it Out p.119-120 and verify their answer by counting folds.

periods.activity_title: Proving Alternate Angles Equal and Chaining Angle Rules

textbook_items_in_class.description: In Fig. 5.25, if ∠f is 120°, find the measure of alternate angle ∠d by first finding corresponding angle ∠b of ∠f, then using the fact that ∠d is the vertically opposite angle of ∠b.
Parallel lines l and m intersected by transversal t. Given ∠6 = 135°, find all other 7 angles using corresponding angles (∠2, ∠4, ∠8 = 135°) and linear pairs (∠1, ∠3, ∠5, ∠7 = 45°).

Lines l and m intersected by transversal t. Given ∠a = 120° and ∠f = 70°. Determine whether l ∥ m by comparing corresponding angle ∠b (= 60°, since ∠a + ∠b = 180°) with ∠f (= 70°). Not equal, so not parallel.
Parallel lines l and m intersected by transversal t. Given ∠3 = 50°, find ∠6 using the interior angle sum: ∠2 = 130° (linear pair with ∠3), and ∠6 = ∠2 = 130° (corresponding angles).

phases.description: Recap the biconditional from Period 6 and construction methods from Period 7. Introduce alternate angles by position (Z-shape) and ask students whether they expect them to be equal.
Activity 6 (p.120) ("in Fig. 5.25, if ∠f is 120°, find the measure of alternate angle ∠d...."). Introduce interior angles and prove their sum is 180°.

Example 1, section 5.8 p.120-121 ("parallel lines l and m; given ∠6 = 135°, find all other 7 angles...."). Then Example 2, section 5.8 p.121 ("given ∠a = 120° and ∠f = 70°, determine whether l ∥ m....").
Example 3, section 5.8 p.121-122 ("parallel lines l and m; given ∠3 = 50°, find ∠6 using interior angle sum...."). Students attempt final steps of Example 4 (p.122) in pairs.
Debrief Example 4 and introduce the Figure it Out exercises (p.123-125) as the multi-step practice set for the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: alternate angles extend the proof techniques begun in section 5.1 and the corresponding-angle criterion from section 5.6. A common error is confusing alternate interior angles with co-interior angles — stress that alternate angles are on opposite sides of the transversal. For self-study, direct students to Example 4 (p.122) to review the full chain independently.

periods.activity_title: Multi-Step Angle Computations on Parallel Lines

textbook_items_in_class.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 5.8 p.123
textbook_items_in_class.description: Find the ten angles marked (a through j) in Fig. 5.30, which shows various transversal configurations with some angles given.

textbook_items_in_class.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 5.8 p.124
textbook_items_in_class.description: Find the angle represented by a in each of four sub-figures in Fig. 5.31 involving parallel lines and transversals.

textbook_items_in_class.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 5.8 p.124
textbook_items_in_class.description: In the two figures in Fig. 5.32, find angles x and y using transversal and parallel line properties.

textbook_items_in_class.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 5.8 p.124
textbook_items_in_class.description: In Fig. 5.33, ∠ABC = 45° and ∠IKJ = 78°. Find ∠GEH, ∠HEF, and ∠FED using parallel lines and transversal angle properties.

homework.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 5.8 p.125
homework.description: In Fig. 5.34, AB ∥ CD ∥ EF and EA ⊥ AB. If ∠BEF = 55°, find x and y.

homework.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 5.8 p.125
homework.description: Find ∠NOP in Fig. 5.35, where ∠LMN = 40°, ∠MNO = 96°, ∠OPQ = 52°. [Hint: draw lines parallel to LM and PQ through N and O.]

phases.description: Recap the three rules from Period 8: corresponding angles equal, alternate angles equal, interior angles sum to 180°. Emphasise selecting the right rule by inspecting angle positions.

Figure it Out Q1, section 5.8 p.123 ("find the ten angles marked (a through j) in Fig. 5.30...."). Then Figure it Out Q2, section 5.8 p.124 ("find the angle represented by a in each of four sub-figures....").

Figure it Out Q3, section 5.8 p.124 ("in the two figures in Fig. 5.32, find angles x and y...."). Then Figure it Out Q4, section 5.8 p.124 ("in Fig. 5.33, ∠ABC = 45° and ∠IKJ = 78°; find ∠GEH, ∠HEF, ∠FED....").

Assign homework: Figure it Out Q5, section 5.8 p.125 ("in Fig. 5.34, AB ∥ CD ∥ EF and EA ⊥ AB; find x and y.") and Figure it Out Q6, section 5.8 p.125 ("find ∠NOP in Fig. 5.35...."). Preview section 5.9 as a visual curiosity.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: Period 8 built the proof and worked-example chains; this period shifts to independent problem-solving practice. A common error is applying a rule without checking whether the lines are actually stated to be parallel — prompt students to identify that condition first. For self-study, direct students to Example 3 (p.121-122) as a model for interior-angle-sum problems.

phases.description: Review and discuss homework from Period 9 (Q5 and Q6 p.125). Address the hint in Q6 about drawing auxiliary parallel lines through N and O.

Present the visual illusions from section 5.9 where parallel lines appear non-parallel due to surrounding patterns. Students observe each image and discuss what causes the perceptual distortion in pairs.
Students verify with a ruler that the lines in the illusions are indeed parallel, connecting the visual experience back to the geometric definition of parallel lines and measurement precision.

Chapter wrap-up: students recall the key definitions and results from the chapter — linear pairs, vertically opposite, parallel lines, transversal, corresponding, alternate, and interior angles — in a teacher-led recap.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition: this period closes the chapter by reflecting on the gap between perception and geometric truth, reinforcing that the formal definitions override visual intuition. No new exercise content is introduced in section 5.9; the period is consolidation-focused. Encourage students to revisit any worked example from section 5.8 they found challenging before the assessment.

goals.anchor_description: Can two straight lines intersect at more than one point?

Observe Fig. 5.5 and describe how the various line segments meet using mathematical vocabulary (point, endpoint, midpoint, meet, intersect) and degree measures. Are line segments ST and UV likely to meet if extended?
Which pairs of lines appear to be parallel in Fig. 5.6 (dot grid with lines a through i)?
Is it possible for all eight angles formed by a transversal crossing two lines to have different measurements? Why or why not?
What patterns do you observe among the four angles formed when two lines intersect (after Activity 1)?
What about five different angle measures — is that possible for the eight angles formed?
Can you draw a line parallel to l that goes through point A (Fig. 5.23)? Describe your method using tools from your geometry box.

goals.anchor_book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 5.8 p.123
goals.anchor_description: Find the ten angles marked (a through j) in Fig. 5.30, which shows various transversal configurations with some angles given.

goals.section_title: Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Paper Folding
goals.anchor_book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 5.4 p.113
goals.anchor_description: Draw some lines perpendicular to the given lines on the dot paper in Fig. 5.10.
Why are lines l and m parallel to each other in the paper-folding construction of Fig. 5.24?

goals.anchor_book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 5.8 p.124
goals.anchor_description: Find the angle represented by a in each of four sub-figures in Fig. 5.31 involving parallel lines and transversals.

items.prompt: When two straight lines intersect, the angles on one side of the intersection that share a common ray are called _______, and they always sum to _______.

teacher_guide.method_one_line: Recall the definition of a linear pair (adjacent angles on a straight line) and the straight-line angle sum of 180°.

what_each_option_reveals.A: Student conflates vertically opposite angles with adjacent angles and recalls 360° (full rotation) instead of the straight-line sum.
what_each_option_reveals.B: Student correctly identifies the name and sum of adjacent angles at an intersection.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Student names the pair correctly but confuses the sum with 90° (the perpendicular special case).
what_each_option_reveals.D: Student recalls the correct sum of 180° but misnames the pair as vertically opposite angles.

teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: ask the student to point to the two angles sharing a ray on their diagram; extension: name one more pair of linear pairs in the same figure and verify the sum.

exercise.description: Can two straight lines intersect at more than one point?

items.prompt: Two lines are said to be perpendicular if the angle they form at the point of intersection measures _______.

teacher_guide.method_one_line: Recall the definition of perpendicular lines as the special intersection where all four angles are equal and each measures 90°.

what_each_option_reveals.A: Student associates perpendicularity with a bisected right angle (half of 90°).
what_each_option_reveals.B: Student recalls an acute angle from another context (e.g., equilateral triangle) without connecting it to perpendicularity.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Student correctly recalls the 90° condition for perpendicular lines.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Student confuses the straight-line angle (linear pair sum) with the angle at a perpendicular intersection.

teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: invite the student to look back at the small-square notation on their dot-paper work; extension: name a real-world object that shows a perpendicular pair and sketch it with the correct notation.

exercise.description: Observe Fig. 5.5 and describe how the various line segments meet using mathematical vocabulary (point, endpoint, midpoint, meet, intersect) and degree measures. Are line segments ST and UV likely to meet if extended?

items.prompt: Which of the following is an essential condition for two lines to be called parallel?

options.label: A
options.text: They must be of equal length.

options.label: B
options.text: They must lie on the same plane and never meet.

options.label: C
options.text: They must be perpendicular to a common line.

options.label: D
options.text: They must meet at exactly one point at 90°.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: They must lie on the same plane and never meet.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Recall the formal definition of parallel lines: coplanar lines that never meet however far they are extended.

what_each_option_reveals.A: Student conflates the visual appearance of equal-length segments with the property of parallelism.
what_each_option_reveals.B: Student correctly identifies both coplanarity and the non-meeting condition.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Student describes a sufficient construction method (double perpendicular) but not the definition itself.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Student confuses parallel lines with perpendicular lines.

teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: ask the student to re-read the teacher note in section 5.3 about skew lines on different surfaces; extension: give an example of two lines that never meet but are NOT parallel, and explain why.

exercise.description: Which pairs of lines appear to be parallel in Fig. 5.6 (dot grid with lines a through i)?

items.prompt: A transversal crosses two lines, forming 8 angles. Because of vertically opposite angle pairs, the maximum number of distinct angle measures among these 8 angles is _______.

teacher_guide.method_one_line: Apply the vertically opposite angle rule to each intersection: the 8 angles form 4 pairs (∠1=∠3, ∠2=∠4, ∠5=∠7, ∠6=∠8), leaving at most 4 distinct values.

what_each_option_reveals.A: Student has not applied the vertically opposite constraint at all and assumes all 8 angles can differ.
what_each_option_reveals.B: Student has partially applied the constraint, perhaps only at one intersection.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Student has eliminated some duplicates but not all four vertically opposite pairs.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Student correctly applies the vertically opposite rule at both intersections to find at most 4 distinct values.

teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: ask the student to list the 4 vertically opposite pairs by name before answering; extension: state whether 4 distinct values are always achieved or only sometimes, and give a specific example where fewer than 4 are distinct.

exercise.description: Is it possible for all eight angles formed by a transversal crossing two lines to have different measurements? Why or why not?

items.prompt: In the parallel illusions shown in section 5.9, the lines appear to be non-parallel even though they are actually parallel. State one reason why our eyes are deceived, and name the geometric tool that can confirm the lines are truly parallel.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: The surrounding pattern (hatching, radiating lines, or background figures) distorts our perception of direction; a ruler (or set square placed across the lines) confirms they do not diverge and are truly parallel.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Recall that visual illusions arise from surrounding context patterns and that measurement with a ruler overrides perception.
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: let the student look back at the illusion images in section 5.9 and physically place a ruler across them; extension: describe in your own words one other situation in daily life where perception can contradict geometric fact.

items.prompt: Two lines intersect forming angles p, q, r, and s in order around the intersection point. Using the fact that angles on a straight line sum to 180°, justify why angle p must equal angle r.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: Since p and q are a linear pair, p + q = 180°; since q and r are also a linear pair, q + r = 180°; subtracting gives p = r, confirming that vertically opposite angles are equal.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Apply the linear-pair sum twice and equate the two expressions to derive the vertically-opposite-angle equality.
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: encourage pair-share — talk through the two linear pairs before writing; extension: alter one premise (suppose the lines are perpendicular) and re-explain what happens to p and r.

exercise.description: What patterns do you observe among the four angles formed when two lines intersect (after Activity 1)?

items.prompt: A transversal crosses two lines forming 8 angles. A student claims that exactly 5 of these angles can have different measures. Using the vertically opposite angle property, explain clearly why this is impossible and state the actual maximum number of distinct measures.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: At each intersection the transversal forms two pairs of vertically opposite angles, so each pair shares a single value; across both intersections there are 4 such pairs, meaning the 8 angles take at most 4 distinct values — 5 distinct values is impossible.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Apply the vertically opposite angle rule at each intersection to show that 4 pairs of equal angles reduce the maximum distinct count to 4.
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: ask the student to label the 8 angles numerically and list the vertically opposite pairs before writing the explanation; extension: construct a specific numerical example where only 2 distinct values appear and explain the extra constraint that caused this reduction.

exercise.description: What about five different angle measures — is that possible for the eight angles formed?

items.prompt: Line t is a transversal crossing lines l and m. At the intersection with l the angle in the upper-right position is 75°, and at the intersection with m the angle in the upper-right position is also 75°. Explain, using the corresponding-angles criterion, whether l and m are parallel.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: The two 75° angles occupy the same positional location at each intersection (upper-right), so they are corresponding angles; because corresponding angles are equal, the biconditional established in section 5.6 confirms that l and m are parallel.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Apply the biconditional: corresponding angles are equal if and only if the two lines are parallel.
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: prompt with 'Which rule says equal corresponding angles means parallel?' and allow the student to re-read the biconditional statement; extension: change the upper-right angle at m to 80° and re-explain what the differing values tell you about l and m.

exercise.description: Can you draw a line parallel to l that goes through point A (Fig. 5.23)? Describe your method using tools from your geometry box.

items.prompt: Parallel lines l and m are cut by transversal t. Angle f (between the parallel lines, on the right side of t, adjacent to line m) measures 115°. Using a chain of corresponding and vertically opposite angle relationships, prove that alternate angle d (between the parallel lines, on the left side of t, adjacent to line l) also measures 115°. Do not use measurement — use only the angle rules.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: Since l ∥ m, angle f and angle b (at line l, same position as f) are corresponding angles, so ∠b = 115°; angle d is the vertically opposite angle of ∠b at line l's intersection, so ∠d = ∠b = 115°; therefore ∠f = ∠d = 115°.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Chain: alternate angle ∠f → corresponding angle ∠b (equal, lines parallel) → vertically opposite angle ∠d (equal to ∠b) to prove ∠f = ∠d.
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: write the three steps as prompts — 'Step 1: find the corresponding angle of ∠f … Step 2: find the vertically opposite angle of that result …'; extension: now prove the same result for a general angle measure x (not 115°) using algebraic symbols throughout.

exercise.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 5.8 p.123
exercise.description: Find the ten angles marked (a through j) in Fig. 5.30, which shows various transversal configurations with some angles given.

items.section_title: Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Paper Folding
items.prompt: A square sheet of paper is folded horizontally in half three times, creating a set of horizontal crease lines, and then folded vertically in half once, creating one vertical crease line. (a) How many horizontal crease lines are formed after three horizontal folds? (b) How many crease lines in total (horizontal and vertical) are there on the sheet? (c) What is the angle between any horizontal crease and the vertical crease?

teacher_guide.expected_answer: (a) After n = 3 horizontal folds, the number of horizontal crease lines is 2³ + 1 − 2 = no — applying the pattern 2ⁿ + 1: 2³ + 1 = 9 horizontal crease lines (including the edges if counted, or 7 interior creases, but the chapter pattern gives 9 lines total after 3 folds including the original edges); the section states 3 folds → 9 parallel lines (using the 2ⁿ + 1 formula with n = 3: 2³ + 1 = 9). (b) Total crease lines = 9 horizontal + 1 vertical = 10. (c) The vertical fold is perpendicular to all horizontal folds, so the angle between them is 90°.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Apply the paper-folding pattern 2ⁿ + 1 for n = 3 horizontal folds to find 9 horizontal lines, add 1 vertical crease, and use the perpendicularity of the vertical fold to state 90°.
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: provide the student with an actual square sheet to fold physically and count the lines; extension: change the number of horizontal folds to 4 and predict the total crease count without folding, using the formula.

exercise.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 5.4 p.113
exercise.description: Draw some lines perpendicular to the given lines on the dot paper in Fig. 5.10.

items.prompt: A student wants to draw a line parallel to a given line l passing through a point P not on l. They use a set square to draw a line n perpendicular to l, and then draw a second line m perpendicular to n through P. Explain why l and m are parallel, referring to the corresponding-angles criterion.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: Line n acts as a transversal; l makes a 90° angle with n (corresponding angle at l's intersection), and m also makes a 90° angle with n (corresponding angle at P); because both corresponding angles equal 90°, the corresponding-angles criterion confirms l ∥ m.
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Apply the corresponding-angles criterion: both l and m make 90° with transversal n, so their corresponding angles are equal and the lines are parallel.
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: offer a visual manipulative — fold paper to physically create l, n, and m and observe the angles; extension: change the construction so that instead of 90°, l and m each make a 65° corresponding angle with n, and explain whether they would still be parallel.

exercise.description: Why are lines l and m parallel to each other in the paper-folding construction of Fig. 5.24?

items.prompt: Parallel lines p and q are cut by transversal t. One of the angles at the intersection with p, on the left side of t above line p, measures 48°. Find: (a) its linear-pair partner at the same intersection, (b) the corresponding angle at the intersection with q, (c) the alternate angle to the original 48° angle.

teacher_guide.expected_answer: (a) Linear-pair partner = 180° − 48° = 132°. (b) Corresponding angle at q = 48° (corresponding angles equal, p ∥ q). (c) The alternate angle is on the opposite side of t between the parallel lines; it equals 48° (alternate angles are equal when lines are parallel).
teacher_guide.method_one_line: Apply linear-pair sum for part (a), corresponding-angles equality for part (b), and alternate-angles equality for part (c).
teacher_guide.inclusivity: Scaffold: draw a number-line style diagram labelling the 8 angles and identify each target angle by its position before computing; extension: change the given angle to x° and express all three answers in terms of x.

exercise.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 5.8 p.124
exercise.description: Find the angle represented by a in each of four sub-figures in Fig. 5.31 involving parallel lines and transversals.
